summary:
The Sabaean Kingdom (c. 900–275 BC) was the most powerful of the South Arabian kingdoms, centered in modern Yemen, whose wealth derived from the monopoly on the production and trade of frankincense, myrrh, and other aromatics that were essential commodities for religious ritual across the ancient Mediterranean and Near East. The Sabaeans controlled the incense routes that connected Arabia to Egypt, the Levant, Mesopotamia, and India, making their small desert kingdom extraordinarily wealthy by ancient standards.

Saba's capital Ma'rib was a sophisticated city featuring the famous [[event:1975|Marib Dam]] — one of the most impressive hydraulic engineering projects of the ancient world, which transformed the arid highland into productive agricultural land. The legendary 'Queen of Sheba' who visited Solomon in the biblical account is widely identified with the Sabaean kingdom, reflecting Saba's fame for wealth and wisdom. Sabaean script — one of the South Arabian alphabets — was the ancestor of the Ge'ez script used in Ethiopia today and spread with Sabaean cultural influence across the Red Sea to the Horn of Africa. The kingdom was eventually absorbed by the Himyarite kingdom around 275 BC but its cultural legacy persisted in South Arabian civilization.
